steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers conclude that landscape variations affect net CO₂ in ways most models are too coarse to capture. To support this, a finding must show that finer-resolution models (1 km²) and coarser-resolution models (10 km²) produce different results for the same metric — proving that landscape detail is lost at coarser scales.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Higher average heterotrophic respiration rates were found for summer months than for winter months when modeled using either a grid of 1 km² cells or a grid of 10 km² cells.: describes a seasonal pattern shared across both resolutions — shows agreement, not a resolution-dependent difference. ✗
  • Choice B — No correlations between seasonal variations in average heterotrophic respiration and CO2 absorption by C. album and other plant species were found when modeled using either a grid of 1 km² cells or a grid of 10 km² cells.: reports that two variables are independent of each other — says nothing about what coarse models miss. ✗
  • Choice C — Lower average heterotrophic respiration rates were found for autumn months when modeled using a grid of 1 km² cells than when modeled using a grid of 10 km² cells.: directly shows that the two resolutions produce divergent results for the same metric, proving coarser grids lose landscape information. ✓
  • Choice D — When seasonal fluxes in average CO2 absorption and heterotrophic respiration were modeled using a grid of 1 km² cells, much more data were generated than when seasonal fluxes were modeled using a grid of 10 km² cells, though the two models reported identical net CO2.: shows the models reached identical conclusions despite using different data — the opposite of what is needed to support the claim. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
